us-politics.headline: US-Iran Tensions Spike as Deadline Looms and Jets Fall
us-politics.body:
Trump warned Iran that 'time is running out' before 'all hell' rains down, while Sen. Graham urged Tehran to 'choose wisely' ahead of a diplomatic deadline. The pressure campaign escalated after Iran downed US fighter jets, prompting Rep. Moulton to declare that 'Iran is winning the war' and former National Security Advisor Bolton to suggest Trump may be in 'panic mode.' World leaders are reportedly moving to manage the Strait of Hormuz crisis independently, bypassing Washington.

On the home front, Secretary of State Rubio revoked the green cards of two relatives of slain Iranian General Qasem Soleimani, who were subsequently arrested by ICE. Supporters call it a firm national security move. Critics argue it sets a troubling precedent of punishing people for family ties. Analysts are divided on what a US endgame with Iran would even look like.

sources.title: ICE arrests relatives of slain Iranian general Soleimani living in US after Rubio revokes their green cards
Rubio revokes green cards of 2 Soleimani relatives amid Iran conflict
Graham backs Trump’s Iran ultimatum as deadline approaches: ‘Choose wisely’
In Iran, what does ‘finish the job’ even mean?
Trump warns Iran: ‘Time is running out’ before ‘all hell’ rains down
Moulton says ‘Iran is winning the war’ after US jets downed
Bolton: Trump likely in ‘panic mode’ after Iran attacks US fighter jets
World leaders bypass Trump to tackle Strait of Hormuz crisis

us-politics.headline: Trump Budget Proposes Defense Surge, Deep Domestic Cuts
us-politics.body:
The White House unveiled a budget blueprint seeking a $1.5 trillion boost in defense spending while proposing sharp cuts to domestic programs. The plan includes a $152 million request to reopen Alcatraz as a high-security federal prison. Supporters say the shift reflects a necessary reordering of national priorities. Critics warn the domestic cuts will hit vulnerable Americans hardest and that the overall direction risks a severe recession.

Separately, former press secretary Kayleigh McEnany cautioned that the 2026 midterms will be 'hard' for Republicans, a rare public admission of electoral vulnerability from within the party's orbit. Cabinet instability adds to the uncertainty, with the departures of Pam Bondi and Kristi Noem fueling speculation about further shake-ups ahead.

sources.title: Trump unveils $1.5T defense surge, deep domestic cuts — what’s on the budget chopping block
America is heading for a recession — and it may be the worst yet
Alcatraz could reopen as a 'state-of-the-art secure prison' under Trump's $152M budget request
McEnany sounds alarm: Midterms ‘will be hard’ for Republicans
Bondi, Noem ousters raise questions about future Cabinet shake-ups
Week in Politics: Trump's latest speech; SCOTUS on birthright citizenship; Pam Bondi

us-politics.headline: Courts and Congress Clash Over Race, Rights, and Redistricting
us-politics.body:
A federal judge blocked a Trump administration effort requiring colleges to certify they do not consider race in admissions, the latest legal collision over affirmative action policy. The ruling adds to a growing list of court checks on executive action in the education space. Sen. Blumenthal dismissed Trump's separate college sports reform order as 'theater, not a fix.'

On redistricting, a new poll shows a narrow majority of Virginia voters support redrawing congressional maps to favor Democrats. Meanwhile, Rep. Eric Swalwell's ongoing clash with the FBI is drawing scrutiny as he eyes a California gubernatorial run. A Hill opinion piece argued that Trump's intervention in California's energy policy over a pipeline dispute signals a broader threat to state sovereignty nationwide.

sources.title: Judge halts Trump effort requiring colleges to show they don't consider race in admissions
Trump crossed an oily line of state sovereignty in California — your state is next
Swalwell-FBI clash draws attention to Trump foe’s California governor bid
Narrow majority in Virginia backs redistricting to favor Democrats: Poll
Blumenthal: Trump’s order to reform college sports ‘is theater, not a fix’
Reclaiming our own birthright: We might need to amend the Constitution

au-politics.headline: Albanese bets on gambling ad crackdown ahead of election
au-politics.body:
Prime Minister Anthony Albanese is pushing ahead with a major overhaul of gambling advertising rules, staking political capital on reforms that have divided his caucus and drawn fierce lobbying from the sports broadcasting industry. The move follows the Murphy report, which recommended sweeping restrictions on how and when betting ads can run.

Supporters argue the changes protect vulnerable Australians from predatory marketing. Critics, including some within Labor, warn the policy risks alienating commercial broadcasters and could prove unworkable. The timing - deep in an election cycle - makes the gamble sharper. Albanese is betting the public appetite for reform outweighs the industry backlash.

sources.title: Trash or treasure? Albanese’s big gamble

au-politics.headline: Pezzullo found reckless in dealings with Liberal powerbroker
au-politics.body:
A confidential report has found former Home Affairs secretary Michael Pezzullo acted recklessly in his communications with a Liberal Party powerbroker, adding fresh detail to one of the more damaging public service scandals in recent years. Pezzullo was dismissed in 2023 after a review found he had used his position inappropriately.

The newly revealed findings sharpen the picture of how those exchanges were assessed internally. The report's existence and its conclusions had not previously been made public. The case raises ongoing questions about the boundaries between senior bureaucrats and political operatives, and whether existing accountability mechanisms are sufficient to police them.

sources.title: Confidential report found former home affairs boss Michael Pezzullo was ‘reckless’ in engagement with Liberal powerbroker

au-politics.headline: Two murder cases grip Victoria as arrests made
au-politics.body:
Victoria Police have arrested two people in connection with the investigation into missing woman Dezi Freeman, whose disappearance in the Porepunkah area prompted a major search. The arrests mark a significant escalation in a case that has drawn sustained public attention.

Separately, a man was charged with murder after being intercepted attempting to board an international flight. Police allege the charge relates to a missing woman at Little River. The two cases are unconnected but together reflect a difficult week for Victoria's law enforcement agencies, both of which are now subject to active criminal proceedings.

sources.title: Man trying to board international flight charged with murder
Victoria police arrest two people as part of Dezi Freeman investigation

au-politics.headline: Victorian election takes shape as Bendigo East challenge emerges
au-politics.body:
Andrew Lethlean has confirmed he will contest Bendigo East at the next Victorian state election, setting up a direct challenge to Premier Jacinta Allan in her own seat. Allan holds the electorate comfortably, but a targeted campaign against a sitting premier carries symbolic weight regardless of the margin.

The move signals that opposition forces are willing to invest resources in seats previously considered safe. Whether Lethlean can convert that intent into a genuine contest will depend on how the broader state political environment shifts before polling day. Allan's government faces pressure on multiple fronts, and a well-resourced local campaign adds another variable.

sources.title: Lethlean to challenge Victorian premier in Bendigo East

world.headline: US-Israeli War on Iran Escalates With Nuclear Plant Strike
world.body:
The US-Israeli military campaign against Iran intensified sharply, with strikes hitting a Tehran university and a projectile landing near the Bushehr nuclear plant, killing one person. The IAEA voiced deep concern over the nuclear site attack. New footage showed US-Israeli munitions destroying an Iranian bridge, while Iranian projectile debris set vehicles ablaze inside Israel.

Trump issued a 48-hour ultimatum for Iran to negotiate a deal, as search teams faced dangerous conditions hunting for a missing US airman downed over Iranian territory. Iran responded defiantly, saying the US and Israel "belong in the Stone Age." Separately, the US arrested relatives of slain general Qasem Soleimani, with Secretary of State Rubio also stripping Soleimani's niece of her US residency.

sources.title: Top diplomat Marco Rubio strips Qassem Soleimani’s niece of US residency
New video reveals moment US-Israeli munitions impact Iranian bridge
Trump says Iran has 48 hours to make deal as search for US pilot continues
UN watchdog voices 'deep concern' as Iran reports new attacks on nuclear plant
US arrests relatives of deceased Iranian ​general Qasem ​Soleimani
Iran says US, Israel belong in Stone Age after Tehran university strike
Vehicles burn in Israel after being hit with Iranian projectile debris
Projectile hits near Iran’s Bushehr nuclear plant, killing one: IAEA
What we know so far about the search for missing US airman in Iran
Rescue team in Iran face 'harrowing and dangerous' search for US crew member
Iran executes two convicted members of banned opposition group

world.headline: Israel Strikes Lebanon Hospital as Ground Invasion Pushes Forward
world.body:
Israeli forces struck a hospital in the southern Lebanese city of Tyre and damaged surrounding buildings, as a ground invasion continued to advance. Witnesses recorded strikes on residential structures in the city. Hundreds of thousands of Lebanese remain displaced, with aid workers describing lives "turned upside down" across the country.

Italy's Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni flew to Qatar to meet the emir and discuss energy security, a visit shaped directly by the regional war's threat to Gulf supply routes. Three suspects in the UK were remanded in custody over an attack on a Jewish charity, a case authorities linked to the broader tensions rippling out of the Middle East conflict.

sources.title: Italy’s Meloni meets Qatar emir to discuss energy issues amid Iran war
Displaced in Lebanon: 'Lives turned upside down'
Three suspects ordered to stay in UK custody over Jewish charity attack
Witness records Israeli strike on building in Lebanon’s Tyre
Israeli strikes damage hospital in Lebanon’s Tyre; ground invasion advances

world.headline: Russia Kills 15 in Ukraine as Zelensky Accuses Easter Escalation
world.body:
Russian attacks killed at least 15 people across Ukraine, including five at a market, as President Zelensky accused Moscow of deliberately choosing escalation over a ceasefire during the Easter period. Zelensky met Turkish President Erdogan, pressing for renewed diplomatic pressure on Russia.

The strikes undercut recent ceasefire signals and drew fresh condemnation from Kyiv's allies. Russia has not publicly responded to the Easter escalation charge.

sources.title: Russian attack on Ukraine market kills five
At least 15 killed in Russian attacks on Ukraine as Zelenskyy meets Erdogan
Russia chose 'Easter escalation' over ceasefire, says Zelensky

world.headline: Afghanistan Quake, Senegal Travel Ban, Alcatraz Plan: World in Brief
world.body: An earthquake in Afghanistan killed eight members of the same family. Senegal banned government ministers from travelling abroad as rising global oil prices strain the country's finances. In the US, President Trump requested $152 million to reopen Alcatraz as a functioning federal prison, reviving a proposal widely dismissed by corrections experts. A fugitive Italian mafia boss wanted for murder was arrested at a luxury villa on the Amalfi Coast.

sources.title: Fugitive mafia boss wanted for murder arrested in Amalfi Coast luxury villa
Senegal bans ministers from foreign travel as oil price rise bites
Trump seeks $152m to reopen notorious Alcatraz prison
Afghanistan earthquake kills eight members of same family

ai.headline: Anthropic Cuts Off OpenClaw, Sparks Backlash From Claude Users
ai.body:
Anthropic has moved to block Claude Code subscribers from using OpenClaw without paying extra, a decision that amounts to a functional ban for most users. The Verge and TechCrunch both confirmed the policy change, which hit the community hard enough to generate a dedicated Hacker News thread. Meanwhile, a separate project called Hybro Hub is pushing in the opposite direction, letting developers run local OpenClaw instances alongside remote agents in an open network.

The timing is awkward for Anthropic. The company is riding high in private markets, with investor appetite strong - but the OpenClaw move signals a tightening grip on how Claude gets used. Hackers are also exploiting the moment, posting a Claude Code leak bundled with malware, according to Wired. A rough week for trust.

sources.title: Show HN: Running local OpenClaw together with remote agents in an open network
Anthropic says Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw usage
Hackers Are Posting the Claude Code Leak With Bonus Malware
Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party
Anthropic essentially bans OpenClaw from Claude by making subscribers pay extra
Tell HN: Anthropic no longer allowing Claude Code subscriptions to use OpenClaw

ai.headline: AI Floods the Web With Fake Content, and Creators Pay the Price
ai.body:
A folk musician named Murphy Campbell found himself targeted by AI-generated fakes of his music and then hit with copyright trolling on top of it - a double blow that illustrates how AI is weaponizing existing legal systems against the people it harms. The Verge documented his case as a warning for independent artists with no legal resources to fight back.

Separately, OneUptime quietly pushed 12,000 AI-generated blog posts in a single GitHub commit, a move that drew sharp criticism when spotted online. The scale of it - one commit, thousands of posts - made the practice impossible to ignore. Meanwhile, The Verge reports a growing push to certify human-made creative work, as audiences and platforms struggle to distinguish authentic content from machine output.

sources.title: A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll
12,000 AI-generated blog posts added in a single commit
Really, you made this without AI? Prove it

ai.headline: Claude Code Finds 23-Year Linux Bug; AI Coding Research Advances
ai.body:
Anthropic's Claude Code uncovered a Linux vulnerability that had sat hidden for 23 years, according to a detailed writeup by Michael Lynch. The find is a concrete demonstration of what AI-assisted code review can do at scale - catching bugs that human eyes missed across decades of patches and audits.

On the research side, a new paper shows that simple self-distillation techniques meaningfully improve code generation quality in language models. Sebastian Raschka also published a breakdown of how coding agents are actually structured, covering the components that make them work in practice. Taken together, the week's developments suggest AI coding tools are maturing fast, even as the business models around them remain contested.

sources.title: Components of a Coding Agent
Simple self-distillation improves code generation
Claude Code Found a Linux Vulnerability Hidden for 23 Years

ai.headline: Meta Pauses AI Contractor After Data Breach Exposes Industry Secrets
ai.body:
Meta has suspended its work with Mercor, an AI hiring platform, after a data breach exposed sensitive information that Wired describes as putting AI industry secrets at risk. The breach is a reminder that the infrastructure supporting AI development - recruiting, evaluation, data labeling - carries its own serious security vulnerabilities.

Also in Meta news, Sarah Wynn-Williams, author of the insider memoir "Careless People," has been legally barred from making negative public statements about the company. The gag order, reported by The Times, comes as her book draws wide attention for its account of life inside Meta. The two stories together paint a company working hard to control its narrative from multiple directions.

sources.title: Author of "Careless People" banned from saying anything negative about Meta
Meta Pauses Work With Mercor After Data Breach Puts AI Industry Secrets at Risk

btc.headline: Bitcoin Market Thins as Bears Face $2.5B Liquidation Risk
btc.body:
Bitcoin is consolidating without clear direction, but the pressure building underneath is real. Five separate data sources point to thinning liquidity inside the market, a condition that historically precedes sharp, outsized moves. Bears are exposed: a push to $72K would trigger roughly $2.5 billion in short liquidations.

Large traders are already bleeding. Wealthy Bitcoin holders lost an average of $337 million per day through Q1 2026. Analysts say the current directionless price action is not calm - it is compression. A breakout, when it comes, could be severe in either direction.

sources.title: Bitcoin shorts risk $2.5 billion liquidation at $72K: Are bears in danger?
Five data sources say the same thing about bitcoin market. It's thinning from the inside
Rich Bitcoin traders lost $337M daily in first quarter of 2026
Bitcoin’s ‘no direction’ action may lead to heavier breakout: Analyst

btc.headline: Ex-UK Chancellor Backs Bitcoin; Tether Eyes $500B Valuation
btc.body:
Former UK Chancellor Philip Hammond has publicly endorsed Bitcoin as a credible alternative to institutions he describes as failing. The endorsement adds political weight to a growing argument that Bitcoin serves as a structural hedge, not just a speculative asset.

Meanwhile, Tether is weighing a fundraising round at a $500 billion valuation but may delay if demand falls short. Separately, a CoinDesk opinion piece argues that corporate digital asset treasuries can no longer justify passive holdings - they need to generate yield or face pressure from shareholders. The message across all three stories is the same: Bitcoin and crypto assets are being held to a higher standard of utility.

sources.title: Ex-UK Chancellor backs bitcoin as alternative to failing systems
Digital asset treasuries must now earn their keep
Tether may delay fundraising if demand falls short at $500B valuation: Report

btc.headline: Kalshi Blocked Again as Prediction Markets Hit Legal Walls
btc.body:
A Nevada judge extended the ban on Kalshi's sports event contracts, rejecting the company's argument that its products qualify as federally regulated event contracts rather than unlicensed gambling. The ruling is a setback for prediction markets seeking to operate across state lines under CFTC oversight.

The legal friction is not limited to the US. Prediction platforms are running into hard resistance across strict Asian markets, where regulators treat event-based trading as gambling by default. Polymarket also pulled a market tied to a missing US pilot after public backlash, highlighting how reputational risk now sits alongside legal risk for the sector.

sources.title: Nevada judge extends ban on Kalshi, rejects event contract defense
Prediction markets are testing legal limits in strict Asian markets
Polymarket takes down market on missing US pilot after backlash
Judge continues Nevada ban on Kalshi sports markets

btc.headline: Solana Faces Quantum Security Tradeoff; Bitcoin Debates Node Conservatism
btc.body:
Solana's architecture creates a genuine dilemma when stress-tested against quantum computing threats. Upgrading to post-quantum cryptography would significantly slow transaction throughput - the core feature the network sells. Speed and security, at least for now, pull in opposite directions.

On the Bitcoin side, developer Jimmy Song is pushing for a more conservative node client, arguing that Bitcoin's strength comes from resisting change rather than chasing it. The two debates reflect a broader tension in crypto infrastructure: how much risk do you accept in the name of performance or progress?

sources.title: Jimmy Song explains why Bitcoin needs a 'conservative' node client
Solana's quantum-threat readiness reveals harsh tradeoff: security vs speed

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Walnut Tart
food.description: A buttery shortcrust tart filled with slow-caramelised leeks, toasted walnuts, and a white miso custard that walks the line between savoury and umami-sweet.
food.tip: Blind-bake the pastry shell for 15 minutes before adding the filling — leeks release a lot of moisture and a soggy base will ruin the whole thing.

exercise.name: The Deck of Cards Workout
exercise.equipment: A standard deck of playing cards, exercise mat
exercise.description: Flip cards one at a time and perform the corresponding exercise for that suit — e.g. hearts = push-ups, spades = squats, clubs = mountain climbers, diamonds = plank seconds — using the card's number as the rep or second count, turning a full deck into a completely unpredictable full-body session.

learn.topic: Taphonomy: The Science of How Things Become Fossils
learn.description: Taphonomy studies the journey an organism takes from death to preservation — why some creatures become fossils while the vast majority vanish without a trace, and how burial conditions, chemistry, and time conspire to distort or erase the fossil record.
learn.why: It fundamentally reframes how we read prehistoric life: almost everything we think we know about ancient ecosystems is shaped by what happened to survive the fossilisation lottery, making it a quiet but radical lens on scientific evidence itself.
